meta.name: 2 Maccabees
meta.copyright: New Revised Standard Version Bible, copyright © 1989 National Council of the Churches of Christ in the United States of America. Used by permission. All rights reserved worldwide.

text.Base: The Jews in Jerusalem and those in the land of Judea, to their Jewish kindred in Egypt, greetings and true peace.
text.KJV: 1:1 The brethren the Iewes that bee at Ierusalem, and in the lande of Iudea, wish vnto the brethren the Iewes that are throughout Egypt, health and peace.
text.DRV: 1:1 To the brethren, the Jews that are throughout Ægypt, the brethren, the Jews that are in Jerusalem, and that are in the country of Jurie, send health, & good peace.
text.NABRE: 1:1 I. Letters to the Jews in Egypt Chapter 1 - Letter 1: 124 B.C. The Jews in Jerusalem and in the land of Judea send greetings to their kindred, the Jews in Egypt, and wish them true peace!

text.Base: In the one hundred eighty-eighth year, the people of Jerusalem and Judea and the council of the elders and Judas, to Aristobulus, who is of the family of the anointed priests, teacher of King Ptolemy, and to the Jews in Egypt, greetings and good health.
text.KJV: 1:10 In the hundreth, fourescore, and eight yeere, the people that were at Ierusalem, and in Iudea, and the counsel, and Iudas, sent greeting and health vnto Aristobulus, king Ptolomeus master, who was of the stock of the anointed priests, and to the Iewes that were in Egypt.
text.DRV: 1:10 In the year one hundreth eighty eight, the people that is at Jerusalem, and in Jurie, and the senate, and Judas, to Aristobolus the master of Ptolomee the king, who is of the stock of the anointed Priests, and to those Jews that are in Ægypt, health and welfare.
text.NABRE: 1:10 Dated in the one hundred and eighty-eighth year. Letter 2: 164 B.C. The people of Jerusalem and Judea, the senate, and Judas send greetings and good wishes to Aristobulus, teacher of King Ptolemy and member of the family of the anointed priests, and to the Jews in Egypt.

text.Base: Since on the twenty-fifth day of Chislev we shall celebrate the purification of the temple, we thought it necessary to notify you, in order that you also may celebrate the festival of booths and the festival of the fire given when Nehemiah, who built the temple and the altar, offered sacrifices.
text.KJV: 1:18 Therefore whereas we are nowe purposed to keep the purification of the Temple vpon the fiue & twentieth day of the moneth Casleu, we thought it necessary to certifie you thereof, that ye also might keepe it, as the of the tabernacles, and of the fire when Neemias offered sacrifice, after that he had builded the Temple, and the Altar.
text.DRV: 1:18 We therefore meaning to keep the purification of the temple the five and twentieth day of the month Casleu, thought it necessary to signify unto you: that you also may keep the day of Scenopegia, and the day of the fire that was given, when Nehemias, after the temple was built & the altar, offered sacrifice.
text.NABRE: 1:18 Since we shall be celebrating the purification of the temple on the twenty-fifth day of the month Kislev, we thought it right to inform you, that you too may celebrate the feast of Booths and of the fire that appeared when Nehemiah, the rebuilder of the temple and the altar, offered sacrifices.

text.Base: One finds in the records that the prophet Jeremiah ordered those who were being deported to take some of the fire, as has been mentioned,
text.KJV: 2:1 It is also found in the records, that Ieremie the Prophet, commaunded them that were caried away, to take of the fire as it hath beene signified,
text.DRV: 2:1 And it is found in the descriptions of Jeremie the Prophet, that he commanded them that went in transmigration, to take the fire, as it was signified, and as he commanded them that were carried away in transmigration.
text.NABRE: 2:1 Chapter 2 - In the records it will be found that Jeremiah the prophet ordered the deportees to take some of the fire with them as indicated,

text.Base: The document also described how the prophet, when he had received an oracle, ordered that the tent and the ark should accompany him, and how he went forth to the mountain where Moses had gone up and had seen the inheritance of God.
text.KJV: 2:4 It was also contained in the same writing, that the Prophet being warned of God, commanded the Tabernacle, and the Arke to goe with him, as he went forth into the mountaine, where Moises climed vp, and sawe the heritage of God.
text.DRV: 2:4 And it was in the same writing, how the Prophet commanded by the divine answer made to him, that the tabernacle, and the ark should follow in company with him, till he came forth into the mount in which Moyses ascended, and saw the inheritance of God.
text.NABRE: 2:4 The same document also tells how the prophet, in virtue of an oracle, ordered that the tent and the ark should accompany him, and how he went to the very mountain that Moses climbed to behold God’s inheritance.

text.Base: The story of Judas Maccabeus and his brothers, and the purification of the great temple, and the dedication of the altar,
text.KJV: 2:19 Now as concerning Iudas Maccabeus, and his brethren, and the purification of the great Temple, and the dedication of the altar,
text.DRV: 2:19 For he hath delivered us out of great perils, and hath purged the place.
text.NABRE: 2:19 II. Compiler’s Preface - This is the story of Judas Maccabeus and his brothers, of the purification of the great temple, the dedication of the altar,

text.Base: all this, which has been set forth by Jason of Cyrene in five volumes, we shall attempt to condense into a single book.
text.KJV: 2:23 All these things (I say) being declared by Iason of Cyrene in fiue books, we will assay to abridge in one volume.
text.DRV: 2:23 and repaired the most famous temple in all the world, and delivered the city, and the laws that were abolished, were restored, our Lord with all clemency being made propitious unto them.
text.NABRE: 2:23 All this, detailed by Jason of Cyrene in five volumes, we will try to condense into a single book.

text.Base: While the holy city was inhabited in unbroken peace and the laws were very well observed because of the piety of the high priest Onias and his hatred of wickedness,
text.KJV: 3:1 Now when the holy Citie was inhabited with all peace, and the Lawes were kept very well, because of the godlinesse of Onias the high Priest, and his hatred of wickednesse,
text.DRV: 3:1 Therefore when the holy city was inhabited in all peace, the laws also as yet were very well kept, because of Onias the high Priest his piety, and mind that hated evils,
text.NABRE: 3:1 III. Heliodorus’ Attempt to Profane the Temple Chapter 3 - Heliodorus’ Arrival in Jerusalem. While the holy city lived in perfect peace and the laws were strictly observed because of the piety of the high priest Onias and his hatred of evil,

text.Base: But a certain Simon... informed the king about the treasury in Jerusalem, that it was full of untold sums of money.
text.KJV: 3:7 Now when Apollonius came to the king, and had shewed him of the money, whereof he was told, the king chose out Heliodorus his treasurer, and sent him with a commaundement, to bring him the foresaid money.
text.DRV: 3:7 And when Apollonius had made relation to the king, concerning the money that he was told of, he calling for Heliodorus, who was over his affairs, sent him with commission to transport the foresaid money.
text.NABRE: 3:7 When Apollonius had an audience with the king, he informed him about the riches that had been reported to him. The king chose his chief minister Heliodorus and sent him with instructions to seize those riches.

text.Base: But when Heliodorus arrived at the treasury with his bodyguard... the Sovereign of spirits and of all authority caused so great a manifestation that all who had been so bold as to accompany him were astounded by the power of God, and became faint with terror.
text.KJV: 3:24 Now as hee was there present himselfe with his guard about the treasurie, the Lord of spirits, & the Prince of all power caused a great apparition, so that all that presumed to come in with him, were astonished at the power of God, and fainted, and were sore afraid.
text.DRV: 3:24 But the spirit of almighty God made great evidence of his appearing, so that all which had presumed to obey him, falling by the virtue of God, were turned into dissolution and fear.
text.NABRE: 3:24 God Protects the Temple. But just as Heliodorus was arriving at the treasury with his bodyguards, the Lord of spirits and all authority produced an apparition so great that those who had been bold enough to accompany Heliodorus were panic-stricken at God’s power and fainted away in terror.

text.Base: For there appeared to them a magnificently caparisoned horse, with a rider of frightening mien; it rushed furiously at Heliodorus and struck at him with its front hoofs. Its rider was seen to have armor and weapons of gold.
text.KJV: 3:25 For there appeared vnto them a horse, with a terrible rider vpon him, and adorned with a very faire couering, and he ranne fiercely, and smote at Heliodorus with his forefeet, and it seemed that hee that sate vpon the horse, had complete harnesse of golde.
text.DRV: 3:25 For there appeared to them a certain horse having a terrible rider, adorned with very rich harness: and he with fierceness struck Heliodorus with his forefoot, and he that sat upon him, seemed to have armour of gold.
text.NABRE: 3:25 There appeared to them a richly caparisoned horse, mounted by a fearsome rider. Charging furiously, the horse attacked Heliodorus with its front hooves. The rider was seen wearing golden armor.

text.Base: But when Seleucus died and Antiochus, who was called Epiphanes, succeeded to the kingdom, Jason the brother of Onias obtained the high priesthood by corruption,
text.KJV: 4:7 But after the death of Seleucus, when Antiochus called Epiphanes, tooke the kingdom, Iason the brother of Onias, laboured vnder hand to bee hie Priest,
text.DRV: 4:7 But after Seleucus' departure out of life, when Antiochus that was called the Noble, had taken the kingdom upon him, Jason the brother of Onias ambitiously sought the high-priesthood:
text.NABRE: 4:7 Jason as High Priest. But Seleucus died, and when Antiochus surnamed Epiphanes succeeded him on the throne, Onias’ brother Jason obtained the high priesthood by corrupt means:

text.Base: When the king had assented and Jason had come to office, he at once shifted his compatriots over to the Greek way of life.
text.KJV: 4:10 Which when the king had granted, and hee had gotten into his hand the rule, he foorthwith brought his owne nation to the Greekish fashion.
text.DRV: 4:10 Which when the king had granted, and he had obtained the princedom, forthwith he began to transport his country men to the heathen rite.
text.NABRE: 4:10 When Jason received the king’s approval and came into office, he immediately initiated his compatriots into the Greek way of life.

text.Base: After a period of three years, Jason sent Menelaus... to carry the money to the king.
text.KJV: 4:23 Three yeere afterward, Iason sent Menelaus the foresaid Simons brother, to beare the money vnto the king, and to put him in minde of certaine necessary matters.
text.DRV: 4:23 And after the time of three years Jason sent Menelaus brother of the foresaid Simon, carrying money to the king, and to bring answers of necessary affairs.
text.NABRE: 4:23 Menelaus as High Priest. Three years later Jason sent Menelaus, brother of the aforementioned Simon, to deliver the money to the king, and to complete negotiations on urgent matters.

text.Base: But Menelaus, when introduced to the king, flattered him... and outbid Jason by three hundred talents of silver, and so had the high priesthood confirmed to himself.
text.KJV: 4:24 But he being brought to the presence of the king, when he had magnified him, for the glorious appearance of his power, got the priesthood to himselfe, offering more then Iason by three hundred talents of siluer.
text.DRV: 4:24 But he being commended to the king, when he had magnified the presence of his power, wrested the high-Priesthood upon himself, over bidding Jason three hundred talents of silver.
text.NABRE: 4:24 But after his introduction to the king, he flattered him with such an air of authority that he secured the high priesthood for himself, outbidding Jason by three hundred talents of silver.

text.Base: About this time Antiochus made his second invasion of Egypt.
text.KJV: 5:1 About the same time Antiochus prepared his second voyage into Egypt:
text.DRV: 5:1 At the same time Antiochus prepared a second journey into Ægypt.
text.NABRE: 5:1 Chapter 5 - Jason’s Revolt. About this time Antiochus sent his second expedition into Egypt.

text.Base: And it happened that, for almost forty days, there appeared over all the city golden-clad cavalry charging through the air, in companies fully armed with lances and drawn swords.
text.KJV: 5:2 And then it happened, that through all the citie, for the space almost of fourtie dayes, there were seene horsemen running in the aire, in cloth of golde, and armed with lances, like a band of souldiers,
text.DRV: 5:2 And it came to pass: that throughout the whole city of Jerusalem were seen for forty days in the air horsemen running hither and thither, having golden stoles, and spears, as it were companies armed,
text.NABRE: 5:2 It then happened that all over the city, for nearly forty days, there appeared horsemen, clothed in garments of a golden weave, charging in midair—companies fully armed with lances and drawn swords;

text.Base: When news of what had happened reached the king, he chosen to think that Judea was in revolt. So, raging like a wild animal, he set out from Egypt and took Jerusalem by storm.
text.KJV: 5:11 Now when this that was done came to the kings eare, he thought that Iudea had reuolted, whereupon remouing out of Egypt in a furious minde, he tooke the citie by force of armes,
text.DRV: 5:11 These things therefore being done, the king suspected that the Jews would forsake the society: & for this departing out of Ægypt with a furious mind, he took the city by arms.
text.NABRE: 5:11 Revenge by Antiochus. When these happenings were reported to the king, he thought that Judea was in revolt. Raging like a wild animal, he set out from Egypt and took Jerusalem by storm.

text.Base: Within the total of three days eighty thousand were destroyed, forty thousand in hand-to-hand fighting, and as many were sold into slavery as were killed.
text.KJV: 5:14 And there were destroyed within the space of three whole daies, fourescore thousand, whereof fourty thousand were slaine in the conflict; and no fewer sold, then slaine.
text.DRV: 5:14 And there were in the whole three days four score thousand slain, forty thousand prisoners, and no less sold.
text.NABRE: 5:14 In the space of three days, eighty thousand were lost, forty thousand meeting a violent death, and the same number being sold into slavery.

text.Base: Not long after this, the king sent an Athenian senator to compel the Jews to forsake the laws of their ancestors and no longer to live by the laws of God;
text.KJV: 6:1 Not long after this, the king sent an olde man of Athens, to compell the Iewes to depart from the lawes of their fathers, and not to liue after the Lawes of God:
text.DRV: 6:1 But not long after the king sent a certain ancient man of Antioch, that should compel the Jews to remove themselves from the laws of their fathers and of God:
text.NABRE: 6:1 Chapter 6 - Abolition of Judaism. Not long after this the king sent an Athenian senator to force the Jews to abandon the laws of their ancestors and live no longer by the laws of God,

text.Base: also to pollute the temple in Jerusalem and to call it the temple of Olympian Zeus.
text.KJV: 6:2 And to pollute also the Temple in Ierusalem, and to call it the Temple of Iupiter Olympius: and that in Garizim, of Iupiter the defender of strangers, as they did desire that dwelt in the place.
text.DRV: 6:2 to contaminate also the temple that was in Jerusalem, & to call it by the name of Jupiter Olympius: and in Garizim, according as they were that inhabited the place, of Jupiter Hospitalis.
text.NABRE: 6:2 also to profane the temple in Jerusalem and dedicate it to Olympian Zeus, and the one on Mount Gerizim to Zeus the Host to Strangers, as the local inhabitants were wont to be.

text.Base: Eleazar, one of the foremost scribes, a man of advanced age and fine appearance, was being forced to open his mouth to eat swine’s flesh.
text.KJV: 6:18 Eleazar one of the principall Scribes, an aged man, and of a well fauoured countenance, was constrained to open his mouth, and to eate swines flesh.
text.DRV: 6:18 Therefore, Eleazarus one of the chief of the Scribes, a man striken in age, and comely of countenance, with open mouth gaping was compelled to eat swine's flesh.
text.NABRE: 6:18 Martyrdom of Eleazar. Eleazar, one of the foremost scribes, a man advanced in age and of noble appearance, was being forced to open his mouth to eat pork.

text.Base: But he, welcoming death with honor rather than life with pollution, went up to the rack of his own accord.
text.KJV: 6:19 But he chusing rather to die gloriously, then to liue stained with such an abomination, spit it forth, and came of his owne accord to the torment,
text.DRV: 6:19 But he embracing rather a most glorious death than an hateful life, went before voluntarily to the punishment.
text.NABRE: 6:19 But preferring a glorious death to a life of defilement, he went forward of his own accord to the instrument of torture,

text.Base: It also happened that seven brothers with their mother were arrested and were being compelled by the king, under torture with whips and thongs, to partake of unlawful swine’s flesh.
text.KJV: 7:1 It came to passe also that seuen brethren with their mother were taken, and compelled by the king against the lawe to taste swines flesh, and were tormented with scourges, and whips:
text.DRV: 7:1 And it came to pass, that seven brethren together with their mother being apprehended, to be compelled by the king to eat against the law swine's flesh, were tormented with whips and scourges.
text.NABRE: 7:1 Chapter 7 - Martyrdom of a Mother and Her Seven Sons. It also happened that seven brothers with their mother were arrested and tortured with whips and scourges by the king to force them to eat pork in violation of God’s law.

text.Base: And when the second was at his last breath, he said, “You accursed wretch, you dismiss us from this present life, but the King of the universe will raise us up to an everlasting renewal of life, because we have died for his laws.”
text.KJV: 7:9 And when hee was at the last gaspe, hee said, Thou like a fury takest vs out of this present life, but the king of the world shall raise vs vp, who haue died for his lawes, vnto euerlasting life.
text.DRV: 7:9 and being at the very last gasp, thus he said: Thou indeed, o most wicked man, in this present life destroyest us: but the King of the world will raise us up which die for his laws, in the resurrection of eternal life.
text.NABRE: 7:9 With his last breath he said: “You accursed fiend, you are depriving us of this present life, but the King of the universe will raise us up to live again forever, because we are dying for his laws.”

text.Base: The mother leaning over her son, fooling the cruel tyrant, she said in her native language: “My son, have pity on me... do not fear this butcher, but prove worthy of your brothers. Accept death, so that in God’s mercy I may get you back again along with your brothers.”
text.KJV: 7:27 But shee bowing her selfe towards him, laughing the cruell tyrant to scorne, spake in her countrey language on this maner; O my sonne, haue pitie vpon mee that bare thee nine moneths in my wombe, and gaue thee sucke three yeeres, and nourished thee, and brought thee vp vnto this age, and endured the troubles of education.
text.DRV: 7:27 Therefore bending towards him, mocking the cruel tyrant, she said in her country language: My son have pity on me which have borne thee in my womb nine months, and gave thee milk for three years, and nourished thee, and brought thee unto this age.
text.NABRE: 7:27 She leaned over close to him and, in derision of the cruel tyrant, said in their native language: “Son, have pity on me, who carried you in my womb for nine months, nursed you for three years, brought you up, educated and supported you to your present age.

text.Base: But Judas, who was also called Maccabeus, and his companions secretly entered the villages and summoned their kindred... and they mustered about six thousand at most.
text.KJV: 8:1 Then Iudas Maccabeus and they that were with him, went priuily into the townes, and called their kinsefolkes together, and tooke vnto them all such as continued in the Iewes religion, and assembled about sixe thousand men.
text.DRV: 8:1 But Judas Machabeus and they that were with him, went in secretly into the towns: and calling together their kinsmen and taking unto them those that continued in Judaism, they brought out to them six thousand men.
text.NABRE: 8:1 V. Victories of Judas and Purification of the Temple Chapter 8 - Resistance from Judas Maccabeus. Judas Maccabeus and his companions entered the villages secretly, summoned their kindred, and enlisted others who had remained faithful to Judaism. Thus they assembled about six thousand men.

text.Base: With the Almighty as their ally, they killed more than nine thousand of the enemy, and wounded and disabled the greater part of Nicanor’s army, and forced them all to flee.
text.KJV: 8:24 And by the helpe of the Almightie, they slew aboue nine thousand of their enemies, and wounded and maimed the most part of Nicanors hoste, and so put all to flight:
text.DRV: 8:24 and a sign of God's help being given, in the forward the duke himself joined battle with Nicanor. And the Almighty being made their helper, they slew above nine thousand men: and the greater part of Nicanor's army being made weak with wounds, they forced to fly.
text.NABRE: 8:24 With the Almighty as their ally, they killed more than nine thousand of the enemy, wounded and disabled the greater part of Nicanor’s army, and put all of them to flight.

text.Base: About that time it happened that Antiochus had retreated in disorder from the region of Persia.
text.KJV: 9:1 About that time came Antiochus with dishonor out of the countrey of Persia.
text.DRV: 9:1 At that time Antiochus returned out of Persis dishonourably.
text.NABRE: 9:1 Chapter 9 - Punishment and Death of Antiochus IV. About that time Antiochus retreated in disgrace from the region of Persia.

text.Base: But the all-seeing Lord, the God of Israel, struck him with an incurable and invisible blow. As soon as he stopped speaking he was seized with a pain in his bowels, for which there was no relief, and with sharp internal tortures.
text.KJV: 9:5 But the Lord almightie, the God of Israel smote him with an incurable and inuisible plague: for assoone as hee had spoken these words, a paine of the bowels that was remediles, came vpon him, & sore torments of the inner parts.
text.DRV: 9:5 But he that seeth all things our Lord the God of Israel, struck him with an uncurable and invisible plague. For as he ended this very speech, a cruel plague of the bowels took him, and bitter torments of the inner parts:
text.NABRE: 9:5 So the all-seeing Lord, the God of Israel, struck him down with an incurable and invisible blow; for scarcely had he uttered those words when he was seized with excruciating pains in his bowels and sharp internal torment,

text.Base: So the murderer and blasphemer, having endured the most intense suffering, such as he had imposed on others, ended his life most miserably among the mountains in a foreign land.
text.KJV: 9:28 Thus the murtherer, and blasphemer hauing suffered most grieuously, as he entreated other men, so died he a miserable death in a strange countrey in the mountaines.
text.DRV: 9:28 Therefore the murderer and blasphemer being very sore strucken, & as himself had handled others, in a strange country among the mountains, with a miserable death departed this life.
text.NABRE: 9:28 So this murderer and blasphemer, after extreme sufferings, such as he had inflicted on others, died a miserable death in the mountains of a foreign land.

text.Base: Now Maccabeus and his companions, the Lord leading them on, recovered the temple and the city;
text.KJV: 10:1 Now Maccabeus, and his company, the Lord guiding them, recouered the Temple, and the citie.
text.DRV: 10:1 But Machabeus, & they that were with him, our Lord protecting them, recovered the temple and the city again:
text.NABRE: 10:1 Chapter 10 - Purification of the Temple. When Maccabeus and his companions, under the Lord’s leadership, had recovered the temple and the city,

text.Base: they tore down the altars that had been built in the public square by the foreigners.
text.KJV: 10:2 But the altars, which the heathen had built in the open street, & also the Chappels they pulled downe.
text.DRV: 10:2 but the altars, which the aliens had set up through the streets, and also the temples he threw down.
text.NABRE: 10:2 they destroyed the altars erected by the foreigners in the marketplace and the sacred shrines.

text.Base: It happened that on the very same day on which the sanctuary had been profaned by the foreigners, the purification of the sanctuary took place, that is, on the twenty-fifth day of the same month, which was Chislev.
text.KJV: 10:5 Now vpon the same day that the strangers prophaned the Temple, on the very same day it was cleansed againe, euen the fiue and twentieth day of the same moneth, which is Casleu.
text.DRV: 10:5 And what day the temple had been polluted of the aliens, it happened that on the same day was made the purification, the five & twentieth of the month, which was Casleu.
text.NABRE: 10:5 On the anniversary of the day on which the temple had been profaned by the foreigners, that is, the twenty-fifth of the same month Kislev, the purification of the temple took place.

text.Base: They decreed by public edict, ratified by vote, that the whole nation of the Jews should observe these days every year.
text.KJV: 10:8 They ordeined also by a common statute, and decree, That euery yeere those dayes should be kept of the whole nation of the Iewes.
text.DRV: 10:8 And they decreed by common precept & decree to all the nation of the Jews every year to keep these days.
text.NABRE: 10:8 By public decree and vote they prescribed that the whole Jewish nation should celebrate these days every year.

text.Base: Very soon after this, Lysias, the king’s guardian and kinsman... gathered about eighty thousand infantry and all his cavalry and came against the Jews.
text.KJV: 11:1 Not long after this, Lysias the kings protectour & cousin, who also managed the affaires, tooke sore displeasure for the things that were done.
text.DRV: 11:1 But a little after Lysias the king's procurator, and cousin, and chief over the affairs, being greatly offended with these things, that had happened,
text.NABRE: 11:1 Chapter 11 - Defeat of Lysias. Very soon afterward, Lysias, guardian and kinsman of the king and head of the government, being greatly displeased at what had happened,

text.Base: And while they were still near Jerusalem, a horseman appeared at their head, clothed in white and brandishing weapons of gold.
text.KJV: 11:8 And as they were at Ierusalem, there appeared before them on horsebacke, one in white clothing, shaking his armour of gold.
text.DRV: 11:8 And when they went forth together with prompt courage, at Jerusalem there appeared going before them an horseman in white clothing, with armour of gold, shaking a spear.
text.NABRE: 11:8 Suddenly, while they were still near Jerusalem, a horseman appeared at their head, clothed in white garments and brandishing gold weapons.

text.Base: They hurled themselves like lions against the enemy, and laid low eleven thousand of them and sixteen hundred cavalry, and forced all the rest to flee.
text.KJV: 11:11 And giuing a charge vpō their enemies like lions, they slew eleuen thousand footmen, & sixteene hundred horsemen, and put all the other to flight.
text.DRV: 11:11 And like lions running violently upon the enemies, they overthrew of them eleven thousand footmen, and of horsemen a thousand six hundred:
text.NABRE: 11:11 Hurling themselves upon the enemy like lions, they laid low eleven thousand foot soldiers and sixteen hundred cavalry, and put all the rest to flight.

text.Base: When these agreements had been made, Lysias returned to the king, and the Jews went about their farming.
text.KJV: 12:1 When these Couenants were made, Lysias went vnto the king, and the Iewes were about their husbandrie.
text.DRV: 12:1 These covenants being made, Lysias went forward to the king, and the Jews gave themselves to husbandry.
text.NABRE: 12:1 Chapter 12 - Incidents at Joppa and Jamnia. After these agreements were made, Lysias returned to the king, and the Jews went about their farming.

text.Base: After the festival called Pentecost, they hurried against Gorgias, the governor of Idumea.
text.KJV: 12:32 And after the feast called Pentecost, they went foorth against Gorgias the gouernour of Idumea,
text.DRV: 12:32 And after Pentecost they went against Gorgias the governor of Idumea.
text.NABRE: 12:32 After this feast, also called Pentecost, they lost no time in marching against Gorgias, governor of Idumea,

text.Base: Judas also took up a collection, man by man, to the amount of two thousand drachmas of silver, and sent it to Jerusalem to provide for a sin offering. In doing this he acted very well and honorably, taking account of the resurrection.
text.KJV: 12:43 And when he had made a gathering throughout the company, to the sum of two thousand drachmes of siluer, hee sent it to Ierusalem to offer a sinne offering, doing therein very well, and honestly, in that he was mindfull of the resurrection.
text.DRV: 12:43 And making a gathering, he sent twelve thousand drachmes of silver to Jerusalem for sacrifice to be offered for sin, well and religiously thinking of the resurrection.
text.NABRE: 12:43 He then took up a collection among all his soldiers, amounting to two thousand silver drachmas, which he sent to Jerusalem to provide for an expiatory sacrifice. In doing this he acted in a very excellent and noble way, inasmuch as he had the resurrection in mind;

text.Base: For if he were not expecting that those who had fallen would rise again, it would have been superfluous and foolish to pray for the dead.
text.KJV: 12:44 (For if he had not hoped that they that were slaine should haue risen againe, it had bin superfluous and vaine, to pray for the dead.)
text.DRV: 12:44 (For unless he hoped that they that were slain should rise again, it should seem superfluous and vain to pray for the dead.)
text.NABRE: 12:44 for if he were not expecting the fallen to rise again, it would have been superfluous and foolish to pray for the dead.

text.Base: In the one hundred forty-ninth year word came to Judas and his companions that Antiochus Eupator was coming with a great army against Judea,
text.KJV: 13:1 In the hundreth forty and ninth yere it was told Iudas that Antiochus Eupator was cōming with a great power into Iudea;
text.DRV: 13:1 In the year an hundred forty nine Judas understood, that Antiochus Eupator came with a multitude against Jurie.
text.NABRE: 13:1 Chapter 13 - Death of Menelaus. In the one hundred and forty-ninth year, Judas and his men learned that Antiochus Eupator was invading Judea with a large force,

text.Base: But the King of kings aroused the anger of Antiochus against the villain Menelaus... and the king ordered that he be taken to Beroea and put to death.
text.KJV: 13:4 But the King of kings mooued Antiochus minde against this wicked wretch, and Lysias enformed the king, that this man was the cause of all mischiefe, so that the king commanded to bring him vnto Berea, and to put him to death, as the maner is in that place.
text.DRV: 13:4 But the King of kings stirred up Antiochus' mind against the sinner, and Lysias suggesting that he was the cause of all the evils, he commanded (as the custom is with them) that being apprehended he should be killed in the same place.
text.NABRE: 13:4 But the King of kings aroused the anger of Antiochus against the scoundrel. When the king was shown by Lysias that Menelaus was to blame for all the trouble, he ordered him to be taken to Beroea and executed there in the customary local method.

text.Base: Three years later, word came to Judas and his companions that Demetrius son of Seleucus had sailed into the harbor of Tripolis with a strong army and a fleet.
text.KJV: 14:1 After three yeres was Iudas enformed that Demetrius the sonne of Seleucus hauing entred by the hauen of Tripolis with a great power and nauie,
text.DRV: 14:1 But after the space of three years Judas understood, and they that were with him, that Demetrius the son of Seleucus with a strong multitude, & with ships was come up by the port of Tripolis to commodious places,
text.NABRE: 14:1 Chapter 14 - Three years later, Judas and his companions learned that Demetrius, son of Seleucus, had sailed into the port of Tripolis with a powerful army and a fleet,

text.Base: Now a certain Alcimus, who had formerly been high priest but had willfully polluted himself... went to King Demetrius.
text.KJV: 14:3 Now one Alcimus who had beene hie Priest, and had defiled himselfe wilfully in the times of their mingling (with the Gentiles) seeing that by no meanes hee could saue himselfe, nor haue any more accesse to the holy Altar,
text.DRV: 14:3 And one Alcimus that had been highpriest, but voluntarily was contaminated in the time of the confusion, considering that there was safety for him by no means, nor access to the altar,
text.NABRE: 14:3 A certain Alcimus, a former high priest, who had willfully incurred defilement before the time of the revolt, realized that there was no way for him to be safe and regain access to the holy altar.

text.Base: The king at once selected Nicanor, who had been master of the elephants, and appointed him governor of Judea and sent him off,
text.KJV: 14:12 And foorthwith calling Nicanor, who had bene master of the Elephants, and making him gouernour ouer Iudea, he sent him forth,
text.DRV: 14:12 Who forthwith sent Nicanor, chief over the elephants captain into Jurie:
text.NABRE: 14:12 Dealings with Nicanor. The king immediately chose Nicanor, who had been in command of the elephants, and appointed him governor of Judea. He sent him off

text.Base: with orders to kill Judas and scatter his companions, and to install Alcimus as high priest of the great temple.
text.KJV: 14:13 Cōmanding him to slay Iudas, & to scatter them that were him, & to make Alcimus high priest of the great Temple.
text.DRV: 14:13 giving him commission, that he should take Judas himself: but them that were with him, he should disperse, & make Alcimus the high priest of the greatest temple.
text.NABRE: 14:13 with orders to put Judas to death, to disperse his followers, and to set up Alcimus as high priest of the great temple.

text.Base: When Nicanor heard that Judas and his companions were in the region of Samaria, he made plans to attack them with complete safety on the day of rest.
text.KJV: 15:1 But Nicanor hearing that Iudas and his company were in the strong places about Samaria, resolued without any danger to set vpon them on þ sabbath day.
text.DRV: 15:1 But Nicanor as he understood that Judas was in the places of Samaria, he purposed with all violence to join battle the day of the Sabbath.
text.NABRE: 15:1 Chapter 15 - Nicanor’s Arrogance. When Nicanor learned that Judas and his companions were in the territory of Samaria, he decided he could attack them in complete safety on the day of rest.

text.Base: What Judas saw was this: Onias, who had been high priest... was praying with outstretched hands for the whole body of the Jews.
text.KJV: 15:12 And this was his vision: that Onias, who had bin high Priest, a vertuous, and a good man, reuerend in conuersation, gentle in condition, well spoken also, and exercised from a child in all points of vertue, holding vp his hands, prayed for the whole bodie of the Iewes.
text.DRV: 15:12 And the vision was in this manner: Onias who had been the high-Priest, a good and benign man, reverent to behold, modest of manners, and comely of speech, and who from a child was exercised in virtues, that he stretching forth the hands prayed for all the people of the Jews.
text.NABRE: 15:12 What he saw was this: Onias, the former high priest, a noble and good man, modest in bearing, gentle in manner, distinguished in speech, and trained from childhood in all that belongs to excellence, was praying with outstretched arms for the whole Jewish community.

text.Base: Then in the same fashion another man appeared... distinguished by his gray hair and dignity.
text.KJV: 15:13 This done, in like maner there appeared a man with gray haires, & exceeding glorious, who was of a wonderfull and excellent maiestie.
text.DRV: 15:13 After this that there appeared also another man marvelous for age, and glory, and for the port of great dignity about him.
text.NABRE: 15:13 Then in the same way another man appeared, distinguished by his white hair and dignity, and with an air of wondrous and majestic authority.

text.Base: And Onias spoke, saying, “This is a man who loves the family of Israel and prays much for the people and the holy city—Jeremiah, the prophet of God.”
text.KJV: 15:14 Then Onias answered, saying, This is a louer of the brethren, who prayeth much for the people, and for the holy citie, (to wit) Ieremias þ prophet of God.
text.DRV: 15:14 And that Onias answering said: This is a lover of his brethren, and of the people of Israel: this is he that prayeth much for the people, and for the whole city, Jeremy the Prophet of God.
text.NABRE: 15:14 Onias then said of him, “This is a man who loves his fellow Jews and fervently prays for the people and the holy city—the prophet of God, Jeremiah.”

text.Base: Jeremiah stretched out his right hand and gave to Judas a golden sword.
text.KJV: 15:15 Whereupon Ieremias, holding forth his right hand, gaue to Iudas a sword of gold, and in giuing it spake thus:
text.DRV: 15:15 And that Jeremy put forth his right hand, and gave unto Judas a sword of gold, saying:
text.NABRE: 15:15 Stretching out his right hand, Jeremiah presented a gold sword to Judas. As he gave it to him he said,

text.Base: When the battle was over and they were returning with joy, they recognized Nicanor, lying dead, in his full armor.
text.KJV: 15:28 Now when the battell was done, returning againe with ioy, they knew that Nicanor lay dead in his harnesse.
text.DRV: 15:28 And when they had ceased, and returned with joy, they understood that Nicanor was slain, for all his armour.
text.NABRE: 15:28 When the battle was over and they were joyfully departing, they discovered Nicanor fallen there in all his armor;

text.Base: Since this is the way the silver matters turned out with Nicanor... I will here come to an end.
text.KJV: 15:37 Thus went it with Nicanor, and from that time forth, the Hebrewes had the citie in their power: and heere will I make an end.
text.DRV: 15:37 but to keep the solemnity the thirteenth day of the month Adar, which is called in the Syrian language, the day before Mardocheus' day.
text.NABRE: 15:37 VII. Epilogue - Compiler’s Apology. Since Nicanor’s doings ended in this way, with the city remaining in the possession of the Hebrews from that time on, I will bring my story to an end here too.

text.Base: If it is well told and to the point, that is what I myself desired; if it is poorly done and mediocre, that was the best I could do.
text.KJV: 15:38 And if I haue done well, and as is fitting the story, it is that which I desired: but if slenderly, and meanly, it is that which I could attaine vnto.
text.DRV: 15:38 These things therefore being done concerning Nicanor, and from that time the city being possessed of the Hebrews, I also in these will make an end of speaking.
text.NABRE: 15:38 If it is well written and to the point, that is what I wanted; if it is poorly done and mediocre, that is the best I could do.
